summary: This source is a PhilPapers bibliography page for David Hume. It consists primarily of a brief biographical summary and a collection of abstracts from secondary academic literature analyzing his empiricism, skepticism, and theories of human nature, rather than primary texts by Hume himself.

principles.statement: All sciences are dependent on the science of man, which must rest on experience and observation.
principles.rationale: To properly understand any field of inquiry, one must first understand human nature through an experimental, observational methodology.
principles.source_quote: all the sciences were ‘dependent on the science of man’

principles.statement: Whatever is conceivable is possible, and whatever is inconceivable is impossible.
principles.rationale: Conceivability serves as a fundamental test for logical possibility in metaphysical reasoning.

principles.statement: Reason alone cannot tell us anything about the world with certainty.
principles.rationale: Human understanding is limited, and causal relations or matters of fact cannot be known purely through a priori reasoning without empirical experience.

mental_models.name: Relations of Ideas vs. Matters of Fact
mental_models.description: A fundamental distinction between two kinds of knowledge: logical/conceptual truths (knowledge of ideas) and empirical claims about the world (matters of fact).
mental_models.example: Used to demonstrate that causal relations cannot be known purely through reason, as they are matters of fact rather than relations of ideas.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on 'occult qualities' or obscure conceptual schemes from ancient philosophy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Terms like 'substance' (as substantial form) or 'faculty' lack empirical grounding and fail to provide genuine explanations of phenomena.
